source_metadata.title_raw: Reed Gallogly - A-19-112 - June 14, 2019 - Santa Cruz

content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
· 1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Reed Gallogly 
Deputy City Attorney 
City of Capitola 
P.O. Box 481 
Santa Cruz, CA 95061 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-19-112 
Dear Mr. Gallogly: 
June 14, 2019 
This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Capitola City Councilmember 
Kristin Petersen regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the 
"Act"). 1 Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of 
the Act and not under any other conflict of interest laws. Also note that we are not a finder of fact 
when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes 
your facts are complete and accurate. If this is not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions 
should change, you should contact us for additional advice. 
QUESTION 
Does the Act prohibit Councilmember Petersen from taking part in governmental decisions 
relating to a proposed development project to construct a hotel on a vacant parcel within the City of 
Capitola's historic downtown given that she leases her residence located within 500 feet of the 
project? 
CONCLUSION 
Yes. The Act prohibits the Councilmember from taking part in decisions relating to the 
project because it is reasonably foreseeable that those decisions may lead to an increase in the 
potential rental value of the Councilmember's leased residence. 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 ofthe California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


FACTS AS PRESENTED BY REQUESTER 
File No. A-19-112 
Page No. 2 
You are a Deputy City Attorney for the City of Capitola and the authorized representative of 
Capitola City Councilmember Kristin Petersen. The City will soon consider a proposed 
development project (the "Project") to construct the Capitola Village Hotel, a five-story hotel with 
89 rooms and 92 onsite parking spaces, on a vacant lot known as the "Old Theater Site" located 
within the City's historic downtown. The City's historic downtown is primarily comprised of 
relatively dense multi-story buildings on smaller parcel sizes, and that the parcels immediately 
surrounding or abutting the Old Theater Site are developed with commercial and multi-family 
residential buildings between two to three stories tall. 
The Councilmember leases her residence, located within 500 feet of the Project, pursuant to 
an annual lease. Along with your request for advice, you have submitted a letter from the owner and 
landlord of the Councilmember' s residence confirming that the lease would not be terminated, the 
rent due under the lease would not change, and the lease's terms would not be modified, as a result 
of the Project. That letter also confirmed that the Councilmember does not have the right to 
, sublease her residence under the lease. You also submitted a letter from a California real estate 
broker whose place of business is located within the City, and that letter sets forth the broker's 
opinions that the Project would not affect the Councilmember's current residential use of her leased 
residence and that residents of the Councilmember's apartment complex "will not be affected either 
positively or negatively" by the Project. 
ANALYSIS 
The Act's conflict of interest provisions prohibit a public official from taking part in a 
governmental decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision would have a material 
financial effect on one or more of the official's financial interests. (Sections 87100 and 87103.) An 
official's interests that may give rise to a disqualifying conflict of interest under the Act are 
identified in Section 87103. With respect to decisions relating to the Project, the Councilmember 
has a real property interest in her leased residence assuming she has an interest in that real property 
worth $2,000 or more (see Section 87103(b)), and an interest in her personal finances and those of 
immediate family members (see Section 87103), based on the facts presented. 
Foreseeability and Materiality 
Regulation 1870l(a) provides that a decision's financial effect on an official's financial 
interest is presumed to be reasonably foreseeable if the official's interest is "explicitly involved" in 
the decision; an official's interest is "explicitly involved" if the interest is a named party in, or the 
subject of, the decision; and an interest is the "subject of a proceeding" if the decision involves the 
issuance, renewal, approval, denial or revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or 
contract with, the interest. Regulation 1870l(b) sets forth the foreseeability standard applicable to a 
decision's effect on an official's interest that is not explicitly involved in the decision, and provides 
that the decision's effect on such an interest is reasonably foreseeable if it "can be recognized as a 
realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical." 
Neither the Councilmember's real property interest in her leased residence nor her interest in 
her personal finances is explicitly involved in decisions related to the Project. 


File No. A-19-112 
Page No. 3 
Regulation 18702.2 provides the Act's materiality standards applicable to a decision's effect 
on an official's real property interest. 2 Subdivision ( c) of that regulation sets forth the materiality 
standard applicable to a decision's effect on an official's real property interest in a leasehold, and 
provides that the effect is material only if the decision will: 
(1) Change the termination date of the lease; 
(2) Increase or decrease the potential rental value of the property; 
(3) Change the official's· actual or legally allowable use of the property; or 
(4) Impact the official's use and enjoyment of the property. 
The facts presented provide no indication that decisions relating to the Project would: 
change the termination date of the Councilmember's lease; change the official's actual or legally 
allowable use of her residence; or impact the Councilmember's use and enjoyment of her residence. 
Therefore, at issue is whether those decisions would increase or decrease the potential rental value 
of the Councilmember's residence. 
The City's historic downtown is primarily comprised ofrelatively dense multi-story 
buildings on smaller parcel sizes. The parcels immediately abutting or surrounding the Old Theater 
Site are developed with commercial and multi-family residential buildings between two to three 
stories tall. The hotel would be five stories tall, two stories taller than the buildings abutting or 
surrounding it, indicating that higher-density development may be permitted in that area 
immediately surrounding it in the future. Therefore, the Project may change the development 
potential of the real properties in the area immediately surrounding it, which includes the 
Councilmember's leased residence located within 500 feet. 
The operation of an 89-room hotel, with 92 onsite parking spaces, on the currently vacant 
Old Theater Site within the historic downtown would alter traffic levels, parking, privacy, and noise 
levels in the area immediately surrounping the Site. Therefore, the Project may significantly 
increase the intensity of use of the area immediately surrounding it. 
A new moderately sized hotel in the City's historic downtown that is multiple stories taller 
than the buildings immediately abutting or surrounding it could become one of the premier 
commercial properties in the historic downtown, attracting visitors and new businesses into the area 
immediately surrounding the hotel. Businesses and potential residents may pay a premium to lease 
real property in that area. Therefore, the Project may change the market value of the real properties 
in the area immediately surrounding it. 
Thus, based on the facts presented, it is reasonably foreseeable that decisions relating to the 
Project may lead to an increase of the potential rental value of the Councilmember's residence 
located within 500 feet of the Project. Accordingly, the Act prohibits the Councilmember from 
taking part in those decisions. 
2 Regulation 18702.5 sets forth the materiality standard applicable to a decision's effect on an official's 
personal finances and those of immediate family members. Subdivision (c) of that regulation, however, provides that if 
the decision at issue affects the official's real property interest, materiality is determined pursuant to Regulation 
18702.2. Therefore, we apply Regulation 18702.2 to determine whether the decisions relating to the Project would have 
a disqualifying effect on the Councilmember's interests. 


File No. A-19-112 
Page No. 4 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
By: 
MFC:aja 
Sincerely, 
Dave Bainbridge 
General Counsel 
~rWI,~ 
Matthew F. Christy 
Counsel, Legal Division

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Does the Act prohibit Councilmember Petersen from taking part in governmental decisions 
relating to a proposed development project to construct a hotel on a vacant parcel within the City of 
Capitola's historic downtown given that she leases her residence located within 500 feet of the 
project?

CONCLUSION: Yes. The Act prohibits the Councilmember from taking part in decisions relating to the 
project because it is reasonably foreseeable that those decisions may lead to an increase in the 
potential rental value of the Councilmember's leased residence.
